request.question: Are Jon Fratelli and Skye Edwards from the same country?

title: Jon Fratelli
Morcheeba
Skye Edwards
The Fratellis
Codeine Velvet Club
Chelsea Dagger
Charles Reeves
Eyes Wide, Tongue Tied
A Heady Tale
Psycho Jukebox
sentences: Jon Fratelli (born John Paul Lawler, 4 March 1979, Glasgow, Scotland) is a Scottish musician and songwriter best known for his work with the band The Fratellis.
He has also played in a band called Codeine Velvet Club, and also performed as a solo artist.
Morcheeba are an English electronic band formed in the mid 1990s with founding members vocalist Skye Edwards and the brothers Paul and Ross Godfrey.
They mix influences from trip hop, rock, folk-rock and downtempo, and have produced eight regular studio albums since 1995, two of which reached the UK top ten.
Their last studio album, "Head Up High", was released in October 2013.
Edwards left the band in 2003, after which the brothers used a number of singers before she rejoined in 2009.
They recruit additional members for their live performances and have toured internationally.
In 2014 Paul Godfrey resigned from the band, thus ending Morcheeba.
Edwards and Ross Godfrey later formed Skye|Ross and released a self-titled album in September 2016.
They continue to tour, on occasion being listed as "Morcheeba / Sky & Ross Trio".
Skye Edwards (born Shirley Klaris Yonavieve Edwards; 27 May 1974) is a British singer-songwriter.
Her career began in 1994 when she and the Godfrey brothers (Paul Godfrey, a DJ, and Ross Godfrey, a multiinstrumentalist) formed Morcheeba, which released five albums with Skye as lead vocalist.
In 2003, the band split, after which Skye released two solo albums: "Mind How You Go" in 2006, and "Keeping Secrets" in 2009.
In 2010, Edwards returned to Morcheeba, again as lead vocalist.
In 2012, she released her third solo album, "Back to Now".
Her fourth solo album "In A Low Light" was released in 2015.
The Fratellis are a Scottish rock band from Glasgow, formed in 2005.

The band consists of lead vocalist and guitarist Jon Fratelli (born John Lawler), bass guitarist Barry Fratelli (born Barry Wallace), and drummer and backing vocalist Mince Fratelli (born Gordon McRory).
Their singles "Chelsea Dagger" and "Whistle for the Choir" were both top ten hits in the UK charts.
Codeine Velvet Club was a Scottish alternative rock band formed in 2008 by Lou Hickey and Jon Lawler, a.k.a. Jon Fratelli from The Fratellis.
The band's debut album "Codeine Velvet Club" was released on 28 December 2009 in the UK and on 6 April 2010 in the US.
"Chelsea Dagger" is a song by Scottish rock band The Fratellis.
It was released as the second single from their debut studio album, "Costello Music" (2006), on 28 August 2006.
It is named after Jon Fratelli's wife Heather, a burlesque dancer whose stage name – a play on Britney Spears – he borrowed for the song.
Fratelli described the tune as "a rock 'n' roll gig in an old speakeasy or something like that."

Charles "Chicky" Reeves is a producer, recording, mixing and mastering engineer working with famous artists such as Prince, UB40, Ray Charles, Tito Puente, Johnny Cash, and Radiohead—as well as MTV's Live at the Ten Spot, VH1 Storytellers, Boyz II Men, Apollo Heights, D-Lite, 247Spys.
In 2004, Reeves moved to London with his New-York-schooled-style.

He continues to collaborate with artists in the studio as well as mixing front of house for Orchestral Manoeuvres in the Dark, Grace Jones, Francesca Longrigg, Scarlett Etienne, Dana Boulé, BEF (formerly Heaven 17), Onetwo, Skye Edwards (of Morcheeba), Prince, Rachael Cormack, UB40, and the Icelandic band Trabant.
His studio, Sublime Studios, is located in Islington, London, England.
His music is published by Extreme Music.
Eyes Wide, Tongue Tied is the fourth album by Scottish rock band The Fratellis.
The album was revealed on 1 June 2015, alongside the first track "Me and the Devil" (which was given away for free from the bands website), and released on 21 August 2015.

The album was written and recorded in Los Angeles, California during November 2014 with Tony Hoffer, who also produced the band's debut album "Costello Music" and Jon Fratelli's solo album "Psycho Jukebox".
"A Heady Tale" is the third single by Scottish rock band, The Fratellis from their second album, "Here We Stand".
It was released on 22 December 2008.
This song is 1 of the 4 tracks on "Here We Stand" to have a heavy use of piano (the other 3 being "Mistress Mabel", "Milk And Money" & "Moriaty's Last Stand").
The B-sides "Mon Yous, Mon us but no' Them" and "When All the Lights Go Out" were also piano intensive.
Jon Fratelli has said that the B-Side "Lonesome Anti Christmas Blues" is the "most depressing Christmas song written".
Psycho Jukebox is the debut solo album by Jon Fratelli, frontman of The Fratellis and formerly of Codeine Velvet Club.
Released on 25 July 2011.
It was recorded with Tony Hoffer (who produced "Costello Music" and mixed "Codeine Velvet Club") in the Sound Factory studios in Los Angeles.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Role and goal: Summarize the first-hop evidence so the next agent can find the missing second-hop information. Produce a single short, factual `summary` that: (1) compresses the grounded facts in the provided passages; (2) exposes the bridge entity, the specific missing fact, or the retrieval direction needed for the next hop; and (3) gives concrete evidence anchors (quoted or clearly paraphrased phrases) that a retriever can use.

Content rules:
- Keep the summary concise and fact-focused. Do not attempt to produce the final answer; describe only what the passages support and what is still needed.
- Preserve the same passage facts and phrasing as you would otherwise (you may quote or paraphrase). Do not change which facts are exposed; only condense them.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from the passages to state a bridge or to justify a next-hop direction. Do not invent facts, infer new temporal facts, or treat composition as unsupported when the necessary premises are present.
- If the passages lack a required premise, or contain conflicting identity links, explicitly state which premise or identity link is missing or ambiguous so the next agent can retrieve or disambiguate it.

Date-format rule (narrow formatting change):
- When the summary includes a date token that appears in the passages (any calendar date or parenthetical date taken from the text), render that date using the canonical form FullMonthName day, year (for example, "September 27, 2017") inside the evidence description. This normalization only changes the string form of the date token; it must not add, infer, or imply any additional temporal facts, nor be used as a reason to produce a final answer.
- Otherwise, leave all other wording and evidence presentation unchanged. Preserve quotations or clear paraphrases of the same passage text; only adjust the date token's string format.

Output contract:
- Return exactly one field `summary` containing the compressed first-hop evidence, the bridge/missing-fact signal, and any retrieval direction needed for the next hop. Do not output final answers, extra metadata, or new fields.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact retrieval query string (single line) in the `query` field and nothing else. The query should be a concise keyword-style search targeted to recover the missing bridge or supporting evidence needed by downstream agents.

General behavior:
- Use the visible facts in summary_1 as anchors (album titles, artist names, dates, relation terms) and compose a focused query that is likely to retrieve passages supporting the needed relation. Prefer concrete anchors and relation keywords over full sentences.
- Do not include explanatory text, user-facing prompts, or requests-for-clarification in the `query` field. Do not invent or assert missing facts (for example, do not supply an artist name that is not present in summary_1).
- Preserve the single-query output contract: emit exactly one string and no other fields or commentary.

Disambiguation for short/ambiguous title tokens:
- If an anchor in summary_1 is a short or potentially ambiguous title token, do not automatically append an entertainment-domain keyword. Only append a plain entertainment disambiguator keyword (for example: film movie album song novel author director) when the original question itself contains explicit entertainment-role words (e.g., film, movie, album, song, directed, author, novella) or when the question explicitly lists multiple title-like tokens. In those cases, include the visible anchor(s) from summary_1 plus the appropriate entertainment keyword to bias retrieval. If the question lacks entertainment cues and does not list multiple titles, do not guess or add an entertainment disambiguator—keep the query anchored to the visible tokens only.

Special-case for missing album artist or genre (narrow semantic boundary):
- If summary_1 shows an album title but does not include the album's artist or genre, or if summary_1 explicitly signals inability to determine genre or explicitly asks for the artist/passage (phrases such as "can't determine" or "please provide the artist"), then emit exactly one compact search phrase formatted as:
  <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
  Use only the visible album title as it appears in summary_1 followed by the three keywords: album artist genre. Do not add punctuation, explanatory words, alternate tokens, or candidate artist names.
- Do not apply this special-case if summary_1 already contains the artist or genre, or if the album anchor is an ambiguous short token that requires human clarification.

Fallback and downstream behavior:
- If retrieval using the emitted query returns no useful passages, the downstream agent may then ask the user for clarification or additional context. Do not ask the user inside the `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Task: Integrate the first-hop `context` with the newly retrieved `passages` and produce a single concise `summary` that preserves the facts needed by the final answer. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when the post-retrieval evidence is genuinely insufficient, ambiguous, or conflicting.

Guidelines:
- Treat a concise answer in `context` as the primary claim. Do not replace that concise context answer unless the `passages` contain a direct, identity-aligned contradiction.
- Before attributing any fact from a passage to the entity or relation named in `context`, confirm identity alignment (clear aliasing or explicit linking). Do not assume two similarly named mentions are the same without such alignment.
- When the context contains a concise bridge claim about a named entity and at least one passage provides an explicit, identity-aligned statement linking that same named entity (or a clearly indicated alias) to the asserted relation, append exactly one brief corroborating sentence that paraphrases that passage's linking fact and references the corroborating passage. Perform an explicit identity-alignment check (alias or explicit linking); do not treat mere name co-occurrence or a passage that merely mentions the name as sufficient. Do not append this corroboration if the `context` already contains the identical corroborating fact verbatim. Keep this addition short and keep the overall summary compact.
- If the `passages` are weak, ambiguous, or conflict with each other or with the context claim (but do not provide an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction), preserve the context claim and append a single short clause that flags the uncertainty or contradiction. When flagging, you may optionally include a verbatim quoted phrase from a passage of up to 10 words to show the source wording. Do not invent resolutions beyond what the `passages` show.
- If the `passages` contain an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction to the context claim, state that the passages contradict the context claim and briefly note the conflicting passage fact (optionally with a ≤10-word quote). Only in this case may the summary stop asserting the context claim as definitive.
- Keep the `summary` compact (aim for 1–3 sentences). Use uncertainty language only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not default to uncertainty when composition across context and passages licenses a conclusion.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from `context` and `passages` to form a licensed multi-hop conclusion; the supporting facts need not all appear in a single passage.
- Do not include long verbatim quotes, do not invent facts, and do not attempt to resolve contradictions beyond reporting them as present in the `passages`.

Output: a single `summary` string that follows the rules above.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Use only the information in the provided summaries as evidence. summary_2 is the later, integrated evidence state and should be the primary source for deciding the minimal answer; summary_1 may contain useful first-hop compression or retrieval-oriented language and may be used to supply premises that, together with summary_2, license a conclusion. Do not invent facts or use outside knowledge.

Emit only the minimal answer string supported by the visible summaries in the `answer` field. Do not include explanations, justification, or any additional text.

Preserve multi-hop composition: you may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts across the summaries to form a licensed conclusion even when no single sentence states the whole relation. Distinguish licensed composition from unsupported inference; emit uncertainty only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting in the summaries.

Minimal-answer formatting rule (explicit):
- If the summaries jointly support one minimal literal and that literal appears verbatim in the summaries and consists of multiple contiguous tokens (for example: a full personal name, a full date, or a place name), emit that full literal exactly as it appears in the summaries (verbatim, unchanged). Do not truncate or coerce it to a single token.
- If the jointly supported minimal answer is itself a single-token label, emit exactly that single token (in lowercase) and nothing else.
- When the summaries contain both a short single-token alias and, in the later integrated summary (summary_2), a contiguous multi-token full-name literal that explicitly identifies or is explicitly linked to the same person (for example via an identity link or biographical framing in summary_2), prefer and emit that multi-token full-name literal exactly as it appears in summary_2. Do not prefer the longer form unless summary_2 provides an explicit identity link tying the alias to the full-name literal; if no such explicit link is present, follow the base identity-link rules and do not force selection of the longer form.

When in doubt about whether the summaries license a single definitive literal, prefer to emit uncertainty only if the required supporting premise or identity link is actually missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not make uncertainty the default when composition or transitive reasoning is required and the necessary premises are present.

Ensure the `answer` field contains only the minimal answer string supported by the summaries (subject to the formatting rule above).

response.hop1_query: Are Jon Fratelli and Skye Edwards from the same country?
hop1_docs: The Fratellis | The Fratellis are a Scottish rock band from Glasgow, formed in 2005.  The band consists of lead vocalist and guitarist Jon Fratelli (born John Lawler), bass guitarist Barry Fratelli (born Barry Wallace), and drummer and backing vocalist Mince Fratelli (born Gordon McRory).  Their singles "Chelsea Dagger" and "Whistle for the Choir" were both top ten hits in the UK charts.

Codeine Velvet Club | Codeine Velvet Club was a Scottish alternative rock band formed in 2008 by Lou Hickey and Jon Lawler, a.k.a. Jon Fratelli from The Fratellis.  The band's debut album "Codeine Velvet Club" was released on 28 December 2009 in the UK and on 6 April 2010 in the US.

Jon Fratelli | Jon Fratelli (born John Paul Lawler, 4 March 1979, Glasgow, Scotland) is a Scottish musician and songwriter best known for his work with the band The Fratellis.  He has also played in a band called Codeine Velvet Club, and also performed as a solo artist.

Chelsea Dagger | "Chelsea Dagger" is a song by Scottish rock band The Fratellis.  It was released as the second single from their debut studio album, "Costello Music" (2006), on 28 August 2006.  It is named after Jon Fratelli's wife Heather, a burlesque dancer whose stage name – a play on Britney Spears – he borrowed for the song.  Fratelli described the tune as "a rock 'n' roll gig in an old speakeasy or something like that."

Skye-Jilly Edwards | Skye-Jilly Edwards (born c. 1972) is an Australian beauty pageant titleholder who won the Miss Globe International 1994 in Istanbul, Turkey and represented her country in the Miss World 1994 pageant in Sun City, South Africa.  She was rated in the top 50 models in Australia by Ralph Magazine.

Psycho Jukebox | Psycho Jukebox is the debut solo album by Jon Fratelli, frontman of The Fratellis and formerly of Codeine Velvet Club.  Released on 25 July 2011.  It was recorded with Tony Hoffer (who produced "Costello Music" and mixed "Codeine Velvet Club") in the Sound Factory studios in Los Angeles.

The Skye Boat Song | "The Skye Boat Song" is a Scottish folk song, which can be played as a waltz, recalling the escape of Prince Charles Edward Stuart (Bonnie Prince Charlie) from Uist to the Isle of Skye after his defeat at the Battle of Culloden in 1746.
response.summary_1: Evidence: Passage states "Jon Fratelli (born John Paul Lawler, March 4, 1979, Glasgow, Scotland) is a Scottish musician" (i.e., Jon Fratelli = Scottish). The only "Skye Edwards"–like entry in the passages is "Skye-Jilly Edwards ... is an Australian beauty pageant titleholder" (i.e., Skye-Jilly Edwards = Australian). Missing/ambiguous bridge: the question likely refers to a different person, Skye Edwards (the singer of Morcheeba), but the passages do not include that individual. Next hop retrieval direction: disambiguate which Skye Edwards is meant and retrieve that person's nationality/birthplace — e.g., search for "Skye Edwards singer nationality" or "Skye Edwards Morcheeba born" — so you can compare that Skye's country to Jon Fratelli's (Scotland).
hop2_docs: Skye Edwards | Skye Edwards (born Shirley Klaris Yonavieve Edwards; 27 May 1974) is a British singer-songwriter.  Her career began in 1994 when she and the Godfrey brothers (Paul Godfrey, a DJ, and Ross Godfrey, a multiinstrumentalist) formed Morcheeba, which released five albums with Skye as lead vocalist.  In 2003, the band split, after which Skye released two solo albums: "Mind How You Go" in 2006, and "Keeping Secrets" in 2009.  In 2010, Edwards returned to Morcheeba, again as lead vocalist.  In 2012, she released her third solo album, "Back to Now".  Her fourth solo album "In A Low Light" was released in 2015.

Morcheeba | Morcheeba are an English electronic band formed in the mid 1990s with founding members vocalist Skye Edwards and the brothers Paul and Ross Godfrey.  They mix influences from trip hop, rock, folk-rock and downtempo, and have produced eight regular studio albums since 1995, two of which reached the UK top ten.  Their last studio album, "Head Up High", was released in October 2013.  Edwards left the band in 2003, after which the brothers used a number of singers before she rejoined in 2009.  They recruit additional members for their live performances and have toured internationally.  In 2014 Paul Godfrey resigned from the band, thus ending Morcheeba.  Edwards and Ross Godfrey later formed Skye|Ross and released a self-titled album in September 2016.  They continue to tour, on occasion being listed as "Morcheeba / Sky & Ross Trio".

The Skye Boat Song | "The Skye Boat Song" is a Scottish folk song, which can be played as a waltz, recalling the escape of Prince Charles Edward Stuart (Bonnie Prince Charlie) from Uist to the Isle of Skye after his defeat at the Battle of Culloden in 1746.

Skye-Jilly Edwards | Skye-Jilly Edwards (born c. 1972) is an Australian beauty pageant titleholder who won the Miss Globe International 1994 in Istanbul, Turkey and represented her country in the Miss World 1994 pageant in Sun City, South Africa.  She was rated in the top 50 models in Australia by Ralph Magazine.

Mind How You Go (Skye Edwards album) | Mind How You Go is Skye Edwards's debut solo album, following her departure from Morcheeba.  Despite anticipation by fans, the album achieved limited success in the UK as a result of insufficient promotion.  It was more successful in other parts of Europe, particularly Poland, France, Italy and Switzerland.

Daisy Martey | Daisy Martey is a singer, songwriter, playwright and screenwriter.  She is the lead vocalist for Noonday Underground.  Late in 2004, Martey left the band to become the lead singer of Morcheeba, replacing their former singer, Skye Edwards.  Martey recorded the vocals for Morcheeba's fifth studio album, The Antidote, which was released in May 2005.  Following the album Martey was fired from Morcheeba, and in January 2006 she sued her former band mates, Paul and Ross Godfrey, for assault, harassment, breach of contract and defamation.
Justine Skye | Justine Indira Skyers, also known as simply Justine Skye (born August 24, 1995), is an American singer, songwriter, actress and model.
hop2_titles: Mind How You Go (Skye Edwards album)
response.summary_2: Jon Fratelli is Scottish. The Morcheeba singer Skye Edwards is described as British (passage 1), so they share UK nationality but the passages do not state that Skye was born in Scotland, making it unclear whether they are from the same constituent country.
